name: Madriguera, El Muyo y Serracín desde Estebanvela
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Ayllón traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, forested areas, and rural villages. The region features varied terrain, including paths through beech forests and routes offering views of higher peaks. Cyclists can expect a mix of open countryside and shaded sections, with some routes exploring areas near natural parks. Best touring cycling routes around Ayllón The most popular touring cycling route is Campisábalos from Galve de Sorbe — circular by Albendiego and the Church of Santa Coloma, a 26.1 miles (42.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 57 minutes to complete, featuring a challenging ascent. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Madriguera, El Muyo y Serracín desde Estebanvela, a moderate 14.7 miles (23.6 km) path, offering views of rural villages and varied landscapes. Local touring cyclists also love the Beech Forest Viewpoint loop from Parque Natural Hayedo Tejera Negra, a 13.1 miles (21.2 km) trail leading through dense beech forest, often completed in about 2 hours 6 minutes. Touring cycling around Ayllón is defined by rolling hills, forested paths, and rural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Ayllón?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes documented around Ayllón on komoot. These routes offer a diverse range of experiences, from moderate to challenging, traversing the region's rolling hills, forested areas, and rural villages.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly touring cycling routes in Ayllón?
A: The touring cycling routes around Ayllón are generally categorized as moderate to difficult, with no routes specifically marked as easy. This means that most routes will involve some climbing and varied terrain. For families or beginners, it's advisable to carefully review the route profiles, focusing on shorter, less elevated sections, or consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1111796446/madriguera-el-muyo-y-serracin-desde-estebanvela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madriguera, El Muyo y Serracín desde Estebanvela&lt;/a&gt; which is moderate in difficulty and offers scenic views of rural villages.

Q: What is the typical length and duration of touring cycling routes in the Ayllón area?
A: Route lengths vary significantly. You can find shorter, moderate routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23749366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beech Forest Viewpoint loop from Parque Natural Hayedo Tejera Negra&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 21.2 km (13.1 miles) and takes around 2 hours 6 minutes. Longer, more challenging routes, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Picoz, Alto Rey Peak – Galve de Sorbe Castle loop from Campisábalos&lt;/a&gt;, can extend up to 77.6 km (48.2 miles) and require over 5 hours to complete.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available around Ayllón?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in the Ayllón area are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1187973522/campisabalos-from-galve-de-sorbe-circular-by-albendiego-and-the-church-of-santa-coloma" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campisábalos from Galve de Sorbe — circular by Albendiego and the Church of Santa Coloma&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23749366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beech Forest Viewpoint loop from Parque Natural Hayedo Tejera Negra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on touring cycling routes near Ayllón?
A: The routes around Ayllón offer diverse scenery, characterized by rolling hills, dense beech forests, and picturesque rural villages. You'll encounter open countryside, shaded sections, and views of higher peaks, especially on routes that venture near natural parks like the Hayedo de Tejera Negra.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Several routes pass by or offer access to interesting landmarks. You might encounter historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4225597" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hontanares Hermitage&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4225602" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Risco de la Cruz de Hontanares&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438613" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entrance to Hayedo de Tejera Negra Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; is a significant natural attraction, and charming villages like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5306654" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Muyo&lt;/a&gt; are often part of the cycling experience.

Q: When is the best time of year for touring cycling in Ayllón?
A: The region's varied terrain, including forested areas, makes spring and autumn particularly pleasant for touring cycling, offering mild temperatures and beautiful natural colors. Summer can also be enjoyable, especially on routes with shaded sections, but it's advisable to start early to avoid the midday heat. Winter cycling is possible but may require appropriate gear for colder conditions.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the touring cycling trails?
A: While specific cafes are not always directly on the trails, many routes pass through or near rural villages where you can often find local establishments for refreshments. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes or those that traverse more remote areas.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Ayllón?
A: The komoot community rates the touring cycling experience around Ayllón highly, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Cyclists often praise the region's varied landscapes, the tranquility of the rural villages, and the challenge offered by the rolling hills and forested paths. The opportunity to explore natural parks and historical sites is also a significant draw.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Ayllón?
A: Many routes start from or pass through villages like Galve de Sorbe, Campisábalos, or Estebanvela, where you can typically find public parking areas. For routes starting near natural parks, designated parking facilities are usually available at the park entrances. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of higher peaks?
A: Yes, several routes provide views of the higher peaks surrounding Ayllón, especially those that traverse more elevated terrain. For instance, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Picoz, Alto Rey Peak – Galve de Sorbe Castle loop from Campisábalos&lt;/a&gt; offers expansive vistas as it climbs towards higher elevations.

Q: What should I wear for touring cycling in Ayllón?
A: Given the varied terrain and potential for changing weather, it's recommended to wear layers that can be adjusted. Breathable cycling attire, a windbreaker or light jacket, and appropriate footwear are essential. Don't forget sun protection, even on cloudy days, and consider padded shorts for comfort on longer rides.
tips: El Muyo is a beautiful town, district of the municipality of Riaza, in the province of Segovia. Known as a ''black'' town due to the slate constructions that give that dark and characteristic color to the village. Although not many people live there, on weekends it is more crowded by sports and nature fans and you can enjoy a good atmosphere. There is a bar at the entrance to the town with a terrace and gardens where you can have a drink. Also noteworthy is the 14th century church with many details of art and historical treasures.

One of the examples of "black architecture" in the province of Segovia, the others are Becerril, Martín Muñoz de Ayllón, El Muyo and El Negredo. The houses owe their dark color to the black slate with which they are built.
